Polynomial and Rational Functions.prompt: This topic covers how quantities change together for AP Precalculus (CED: 1.1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.1). Students explore the fundamental idea that when one quantity changes, related quantities change in tandem. Key concepts: identifying independent and dependent variables in a relationship, describing how changes in one variable affect another, recognizing that functions model these co-varying relationships. Students analyze how input and output values change together using tables, graphs, and verbal descriptions. This sets the foundation for understanding rates of change and function behavior throughout the course. Connections: this concept extends to all function types studied in AP Precalculus (polynomial, exponential, trigonometric, parametric). AP exam note: students should describe co-variation in context, specifying which quantities increase/decrease together and at what relative rates. Vocabulary: co-variation, independent variable, dependent variable, input, output, relationship, function.

This topic covers rates of change for AP Precalculus (CED: 1.2.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.2). The average rate of change of f on [a,b] is [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a), representing the slope of the secant line. Key concepts: interpreting average rate of change in context with units; positive rate means output increases as input increases, negative means output decreases; the average rate of change gives the constant rate that would produce the same net change over the interval. Students should compute and interpret average rates from functions, tables, and graphs. Comparing rates over different intervals reveals how the function's behavior changes. This concept prepares students for the derivative in calculus. AP exam note: students must interpret rates of change in context with appropriate units and explain what the rate means in the given scenario. Vocabulary: average rate of change, secant line, slope, interval, units, positive, negative, context.

Polynomial and Rational Functions.skill: Rates of Change in Linear and Quadratic Functions
Polynomial and Rational Functions.prompt: This topic covers rates of change in linear and quadratic functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 1.3.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.3). Linear functions have constant rate of change (slope): f(x) = mx + b, average rate of change = m over any interval. Quadratic functions have a linearly changing rate of change: the average rate of change over equal-length intervals changes by a constant amount (second differences are constant). Key concepts: for linear functions, the rate of change is the same over every interval; for quadratic functions, the rate of change itself changes at a constant rate; this distinction characterizes each function type. Students should identify linear vs. quadratic behavior from tables by examining first and second differences. AP exam note: students may need to determine whether data is best modeled by a linear or quadratic function based on rate of change patterns. Vocabulary: constant rate of change, slope, first differences, second differences, linear, quadratic, characterize.

Polynomial and Rational Functions.skill: Polynomial Functions and Rates of Change
Polynomial and Rational Functions.prompt: This topic covers polynomial functions and their rates of change for AP Precalculus (CED: 1.4.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.4). A polynomial of degree n has the form f(x) = a_n*x^n + ... + a_1*x + a_0. Key concepts: the degree determines the polynomial's general behavior; higher-degree polynomials can have more turning points (at most n-1 for degree n); the rate of change of a polynomial of degree n behaves like a polynomial of degree n-1; local extrema occur where the rate of change is zero. Students analyze polynomial behavior through rates of change: where the function is increasing (positive rate) and decreasing (negative rate), and how the rate itself changes. Connections to calculus: the rate of change function is the derivative. AP exam note: students should identify polynomial degree from graphs (counting turning points) and from rate of change behavior. Vocabulary: polynomial, degree, turning point, local extremum, increasing, decreasing, rate of change behavior.

This topic covers complex zeros of polynomials for AP Precalculus (CED: 1.5.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.5). The Fundamental Theorem of Algebra: a degree-n polynomial has exactly n zeros (counting multiplicity) in the complex numbers. Key concepts: real zeros correspond to x-intercepts; complex zeros come in conjugate pairs (if a+bi is a zero, so is a-bi) for polynomials with real coefficients; a polynomial can be factored as a_n(x-r_1)(x-r_2)...(x-r_n) over the complex numbers; multiplicity affects graph behavior (odd multiplicity: crosses x-axis; even multiplicity: touches and turns). Students should find zeros by factoring, the Rational Root Theorem, and synthetic/polynomial division. AP exam note: students should understand how complex zeros affect the factored form and graph behavior, even when complex zeros cannot be seen on the real graph. Vocabulary: zero, root, complex number, conjugate pair, multiplicity, Fundamental Theorem of Algebra, factor.

This topic covers end behavior of polynomials for AP Precalculus (CED: 1.6.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.6). End behavior is determined by the leading term a_n*x^n: for even degree with positive leading coefficient, both ends go to +infinity; for even degree with negative leading coefficient, both go to -infinity; for odd degree with positive leading coefficient, left goes to -infinity and right to +infinity; for odd with negative, left to +infinity and right to -infinity. Key concepts: end behavior depends only on degree and leading coefficient sign; limit notation describes end behavior (lim[x->inf] f(x) and lim[x->-inf] f(x)); as |x| grows large, the leading term dominates. Students should determine end behavior from equations and graphs. AP exam note: students should use limit notation to describe end behavior and connect it to the degree and leading coefficient. Vocabulary: end behavior, leading term, leading coefficient, degree, limit at infinity, dominates.

This topic covers end behavior of rational functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 1.7.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.7). For f(x) = p(x)/q(x): if deg(p) < deg(q), the horizontal asymptote is y = 0; if deg(p) = deg(q), the HA is y = (leading coefficient of p)/(leading coefficient of q); if deg(p) = deg(q) + 1, there is a slant (oblique) asymptote found by polynomial division; if deg(p) > deg(q) + 1, no horizontal or slant asymptote. Key concepts: horizontal asymptotes describe end behavior (limit at infinity); a function can cross its horizontal asymptote for finite x; slant asymptotes describe linear end behavior. Students should determine end behavior analytically by dividing by the highest power of x or performing polynomial division. AP exam note: end behavior questions require limit notation or asymptote equations. Vocabulary: rational function, horizontal asymptote, slant asymptote, end behavior, degree comparison, limit.

This topic covers zeros of rational functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 1.8.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.8). The zeros of f(x) = p(x)/q(x) occur where p(x) = 0 and q(x) != 0. Key concepts: zeros are x-intercepts of the graph; a factor in both numerator and denominator creates a hole (not a zero or asymptote); to find zeros, factor the numerator and solve p(x) = 0, then verify the denominator is nonzero at each solution. The multiplicity of a zero affects graph behavior: odd multiplicity means the function crosses the x-axis, even multiplicity means it touches and turns. Students should find and classify zeros from both factored and expanded forms. AP exam note: students must carefully distinguish zeros from holes by checking denominator values. Vocabulary: zero, x-intercept, numerator, denominator, factor, hole, multiplicity, rational function.

This topic covers vertical asymptotes of rational functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 1.9.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.9). Vertical asymptotes of f(x) = p(x)/q(x) occur at x = c where q(c) = 0 but p(c) != 0 (after canceling common factors). Near a vertical asymptote, the function grows without bound: the output approaches +infinity or -infinity. Key concepts: the sign of f(x) near the asymptote (from left and right) depends on the signs of p(x) and q(x); odd multiplicity of the factor in the denominator means the function goes to opposite infinities on each side; even multiplicity means the same infinity on both sides. Students should find vertical asymptotes by factoring the denominator and checking for common factors. AP exam note: students should describe behavior near vertical asymptotes using limit notation and sign analysis. Vocabulary: vertical asymptote, unbounded, infinity, sign analysis, multiplicity, factor, denominator.

This topic covers holes in rational functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 1.10.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.10). A hole occurs at x = c when (x-c) is a factor of both the numerator and denominator. After canceling the common factor, the simplified function value at c gives the y-coordinate of the hole. Key concepts: holes are removable discontinuities; the function is undefined at the hole but has a limit there; graphically, a hole appears as an open circle; to find holes: factor both numerator and denominator, identify common factors, cancel and evaluate. Distinguishing holes from vertical asymptotes: common factors create holes; factors only in the denominator create asymptotes. AP exam note: students must identify the location (x,y) of holes and distinguish them from asymptotes and zeros. Vocabulary: hole, removable discontinuity, common factor, cancel, simplified function, open circle, undefined.

Polynomial and Rational Functions.skill: Equivalent Representations of Polynomial and Rational Expressions
Polynomial and Rational Functions.prompt: This topic covers equivalent forms of polynomial and rational expressions for AP Precalculus (CED: 1.11.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.11). Students convert between different representations: standard form, factored form, and forms obtained through polynomial division. Key concepts: factored form reveals zeros and multiplicities; standard form shows degree and leading coefficient for end behavior; polynomial long division or synthetic division converts improper rational expressions to mixed form (polynomial + proper fraction), revealing slant asymptotes; equivalent forms provide different information about the same function. Students should be fluent in converting between forms and choosing the most useful representation for a given question. AP exam note: different representations highlight different features; students should know which form answers which question (zeros, end behavior, asymptotes, intercepts). Vocabulary: equivalent representations, factored form, standard form, polynomial division, proper fraction, convert.

This topic covers function transformations for AP Precalculus (CED: 1.12.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.12). Transformations modify a parent function: vertical shift (f(x) + k), horizontal shift (f(x-h)), vertical stretch/compression (a*f(x)), horizontal stretch/compression (f(bx)), and reflection (over x-axis: -f(x), over y-axis: f(-x)). Key concepts: inside transformations (affecting x) act opposite to expectation (f(x-2) shifts right); outside transformations (affecting y) act as expected; multiple transformations apply in a specific order; transformations preserve the general shape while changing position, size, or orientation. Students should describe transformations from equations and apply them to graph parent functions. AP exam note: transformation questions may involve any function type. Students should be able to write the equation of a transformed function and describe transformations verbally. Vocabulary: transformation, shift, stretch, compression, reflection, parent function, vertical, horizontal.

Polynomial and Rational Functions.skill: Function Model Selection and Assumption Articulation
Polynomial and Rational Functions.prompt: This topic covers selecting appropriate function models for AP Precalculus (CED: 1.13.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.13). Students learn to choose among linear, quadratic, polynomial, rational, and other models based on data patterns and contextual information. Key concepts: examine rates of change (constant = linear, linearly changing = quadratic); look for asymptotic behavior suggesting rational functions; consider the context and physical constraints; articulate assumptions underlying each model choice (domain restrictions, continuity, long-term behavior). Students should justify their model selection by explaining how the data or context aligns with the properties of the chosen function type. AP exam note: model selection requires reasoning about why a particular function type fits the data or scenario. Students must articulate assumptions and limitations. Vocabulary: model selection, assumptions, linear, quadratic, polynomial, rational, context, justification.

This topic covers constructing and applying function models for AP Precalculus (CED: 1.14.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.14). Students build mathematical models from data or contextual descriptions, then use them to make predictions. Key concepts: using data points to determine parameters (e.g., finding coefficients of a polynomial through system of equations); using technology for regression; interpreting model predictions in context; understanding model limitations (interpolation vs. extrapolation, domain restrictions). Students should construct models for polynomial and rational function contexts and evaluate the reasonableness of predictions. Connections: model construction skills apply to all function families studied in AP Precalculus. AP exam note: students must construct models, use them for predictions, and discuss the validity and limitations of their models in context. Vocabulary: model construction, regression, parameters, prediction, interpolation, extrapolation, limitations, context.

Exponential and Logarithmic Functions.skill: Change in Arithmetic and Geometric Sequences
Exponential and Logarithmic Functions.prompt: This topic covers arithmetic and geometric sequences for AP Precalculus (CED: 2.1.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.1). An arithmetic sequence has a constant common difference d: a_n = a_1 + (n-1)d. A geometric sequence has a constant common ratio r: a_n = a_1 * r^(n-1). Key concepts: arithmetic sequences model additive change (constant amount added); geometric sequences model multiplicative change (constant factor applied); identifying which type from data (constant first differences = arithmetic, constant ratios = geometric); these sequences are the discrete analogs of linear and exponential functions respectively. Students should write explicit and recursive formulas for both types. AP exam note: students must distinguish arithmetic from geometric patterns and connect them to their continuous counterparts (linear and exponential functions). Vocabulary: arithmetic sequence, geometric sequence, common difference, common ratio, explicit formula, recursive formula, additive, multiplicative.

Exponential and Logarithmic Functions.skill: Change in Linear and Exponential Functions
Exponential and Logarithmic Functions.prompt: This topic covers the relationship between linear and exponential functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 2.2.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.2). Linear functions have constant additive rate of change: f(x) = mx + b, adding m for each unit increase in x. Exponential functions have constant multiplicative rate of change: f(x) = a*b^x, multiplying by b for each unit increase in x. Key concepts: over equal-length input intervals, linear functions change by a constant amount while exponential functions change by a constant factor; this fundamental distinction determines long-term behavior (exponential growth eventually exceeds any linear growth). Students should identify linear vs. exponential behavior from tables (constant differences vs. constant ratios) and contextual descriptions. AP exam note: distinguishing linear from exponential growth is a foundational precalculus skill tested throughout the course. Vocabulary: linear, exponential, additive change, multiplicative change, constant difference, constant ratio, growth.

This topic covers exponential functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 2.3.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.3). An exponential function has the form f(x) = a*b^x where a != 0, b > 0, b != 1. Key properties: the domain is all real numbers, range is (0,inf) if a > 0 or (-inf,0) if a < 0; the function is always increasing (b > 1) or always decreasing (0 < b < 1); there is a horizontal asymptote at y = 0; the y-intercept is (0,a); the function never equals zero. The base b determines growth or decay rate; the initial value a determines the vertical stretch and direction. Students should graph exponential functions, identify key features, and understand the effect of parameters a and b. AP exam note: students must identify and interpret parameters of exponential functions in context. Vocabulary: exponential function, base, initial value, growth, decay, horizontal asymptote, domain, range.

This topic covers manipulating exponential expressions for AP Precalculus (CED: 2.4.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.4). Students rewrite exponential expressions using properties of exponents: a^m * a^n = a^(m+n), a^m/a^n = a^(m-n), (a^m)^n = a^(mn), a^0 = 1, a^(-n) = 1/a^n. Key applications: converting between different bases (b^x = e^(x*ln(b))); expressing growth rates in different time units (if f doubles every 3 years, f = a*2^(t/3)); rewriting to reveal different information about the function. Students should be fluent in transforming exponential expressions to equivalent forms. AP exam note: manipulation skills are essential for solving exponential equations and comparing growth rates across different representations. Vocabulary: exponent properties, base conversion, equivalent forms, rewrite, growth rate, time unit conversion.

Exponential and Logarithmic Functions.skill: Exponential Function Context and Data Modeling
Exponential and Logarithmic Functions.prompt: This topic covers exponential modeling for AP Precalculus (CED: 2.5.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.5). Students use exponential functions to model real-world scenarios: population growth, radioactive decay, compound interest, depreciation. Key concepts: determining the initial value and growth/decay factor from data or context; using two data points to find the exponential function f(x) = a*b^x; applying the model to make predictions; understanding the meaning of the base (growth factor per unit time) and interpreting it in context. Students should use technology for exponential regression when appropriate. Limitations: exponential models may not account for limiting factors (carrying capacity) or changing rates. AP exam note: students must construct exponential models from data or descriptions and interpret parameters and predictions in context. Vocabulary: exponential model, initial value, growth factor, decay factor, regression, prediction, context, limitation.

This topic covers comparing and validating competing function models for AP Precalculus (CED: 2.6.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.6). When multiple function types could model the same data, students must evaluate which model is most appropriate. Key concepts: examining residuals (deviations from model predictions) for patterns suggesting a better model; comparing predicted vs. actual values; considering the context to determine which function type makes physical sense; understanding that a good fit over the observed data does not guarantee good predictions outside that range. Students should compare linear, quadratic, and exponential models and justify their selection. AP exam note: model validation questions require students to explain why one model is superior using both mathematical evidence and contextual reasoning. Vocabulary: model validation, residual, competing models, best fit, prediction, context, justification, comparison.

This topic covers function composition for AP Precalculus (CED: 2.7.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.7). The composition (f o g)(x) = f(g(x)) applies g first, then f to the result. Key concepts: the output of the inner function becomes the input of the outer function; the domain of f o g is the set of x in the domain of g such that g(x) is in the domain of f; composition is not commutative (f o g != g o f in general); decomposing a complex function into a composition of simpler functions. Students should evaluate compositions from formulas, tables, and graphs. Composition connects to many advanced topics: the Chain Rule in calculus, inverse functions (f o f^(-1) = identity), and modeling multi-step processes. AP exam note: students may need to evaluate, interpret, or decompose compositions in various representations. Vocabulary: composition, inner function, outer function, domain, decompose, order of operations, not commutative.

This topic covers inverse functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 2.8.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.8). The inverse f^(-1) reverses f: if f(a) = b, then f^(-1)(b) = a. A function has an inverse if and only if it is one-to-one (passes the horizontal line test). Key concepts: f(f^(-1)(x)) = x and f^(-1)(f(x)) = x (composition equals identity); the graph of f^(-1) is the reflection of f's graph over y = x; domain of f^(-1) = range of f, and vice versa; to find f^(-1) algebraically, swap x and y in y = f(x) and solve for y. Restricting the domain of a non-one-to-one function can create an invertible function. AP exam note: students should find inverses algebraically, graphically, and from tables. Understanding inverse relationships is essential for logarithms (inverse of exponentials) and inverse trigonometric functions. Vocabulary: inverse function, one-to-one, horizontal line test, reflection, domain, range, identity composition.

This topic covers logarithmic expressions for AP Precalculus (CED: 2.9.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.9). The logarithm log_b(x) = y means b^y = x. Key properties: log_b(b^x) = x and b^(log_b(x)) = x; log_b(mn) = log_b(m) + log_b(n); log_b(m/n) = log_b(m) - log_b(n); log_b(m^n) = n*log_b(m); log_b(1) = 0; log_b(b) = 1; change of base: log_b(x) = ln(x)/ln(b). Common logarithms: log (base 10) and ln (base e). Students should evaluate logarithmic expressions, expand and condense using properties, and convert between exponential and logarithmic forms. AP exam note: logarithm properties are essential for solving exponential equations and for the semi-log plot analysis in Topic 2.15. Vocabulary: logarithm, base, exponent, properties, expand, condense, change of base, common log, natural log.

This topic covers logarithms as inverses of exponential functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 2.10.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.10). Since f(x) = b^x is one-to-one, its inverse f^(-1)(x) = log_b(x) exists. Key concepts: the logarithm undoes exponentiation (log_b(b^x) = x) and exponentiation undoes logarithm (b^(log_b(x)) = x); the graph of y = log_b(x) is the reflection of y = b^x over y = x; domain of log_b = (0,inf), range = all reals; the logarithm has a vertical asymptote at x = 0 and passes through (1,0). For b > 1, log_b is increasing; for 0 < b < 1, decreasing. Students should graph logarithmic functions and connect their features to the corresponding exponential. AP exam note: understanding the inverse relationship between exponentials and logarithms is essential for solving equations and modeling. Vocabulary: inverse, exponential, logarithm, reflection, domain, range, vertical asymptote, one-to-one.

This topic covers logarithmic functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 2.11.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.11). The function f(x) = log_b(x) has domain (0,inf), range all reals, vertical asymptote x = 0, x-intercept (1,0), and passes through (b,1). Key features: logarithmic growth is slow (much slower than polynomial or exponential); the function is concave down (rate of change is decreasing); for b > 1, the function is increasing. Transformations: a*log_b(cx+d) + k shifts, stretches, and reflects the parent function. Students should graph logarithmic functions with transformations and identify key features (domain, range, asymptote, intercepts). AP exam note: logarithmic functions model phenomena with diminishing returns (decibels, pH, Richter scale). Students should connect features to context. Vocabulary: logarithmic function, domain, range, vertical asymptote, concave down, diminishing returns, transformation.

This topic covers manipulating logarithmic expressions for AP Precalculus (CED: 2.12.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.12). Key properties: log(mn) = log(m)+log(n); log(m/n) = log(m)-log(n); log(m^n) = n*log(m); change of base: log_b(x) = log(x)/log(b). Students use these properties to: expand logarithmic expressions (useful for differentiation in calculus), condense logarithmic expressions (useful for solving equations), and convert between bases. Students should simplify complex logarithmic expressions and verify equivalence of different forms. AP exam note: logarithm manipulation is tested directly and is essential for solving logarithmic and exponential equations. Students should be fluent with all properties. Vocabulary: logarithm properties, expand, condense, Product Rule, Quotient Rule, Power Rule, change of base.

Exponential and Logarithmic Functions.skill: Exponential and Logarithmic Equations and Inequalities
Exponential and Logarithmic Functions.prompt: This topic covers solving exponential and logarithmic equations for AP Precalculus (CED: 2.13.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.13). For exponential equations: isolate the exponential term, take logarithm of both sides, solve. Example: 3^(2x) = 15 gives 2x = log_3(15), x = log(15)/(2log(3)). For logarithmic equations: condense logs, convert to exponential form, solve, and check for extraneous solutions (arguments must be positive). Inequalities require careful attention to direction when multiplying/dividing by negative values or when the base is between 0 and 1. Students should solve both simple and complex equations and verify solutions in the original equation. AP exam note: extraneous solutions are a common pitfall in logarithmic equations. Students must check that solutions are in the domain. Vocabulary: exponential equation, logarithmic equation, extraneous solution, domain check, isolate, convert, inequality.

Exponential and Logarithmic Functions.skill: Logarithmic Function Context and Data Modeling
Exponential and Logarithmic Functions.prompt: This topic covers logarithmic modeling for AP Precalculus (CED: 2.14.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.14). Logarithmic functions model phenomena with rapid initial change that slows over time: sound intensity (decibels), earthquake magnitude (Richter scale), acidity (pH), information entropy. Key concepts: logarithmic models are appropriate when the rate of change is inversely proportional to the input; building models from data using logarithmic regression; interpreting parameters in context; understanding limitations (logarithmic models may not capture long-term behavior). Students should construct logarithmic models, make predictions, and compare with other model types. AP exam note: students must justify why a logarithmic model is appropriate for given data and interpret the model in context. Vocabulary: logarithmic model, diminishing returns, regression, context, rate of change, prediction, limitation.

This topic covers semi-log plots for AP Precalculus (CED: 2.15.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.15). A semi-log plot uses a logarithmic scale on the y-axis and a linear scale on the x-axis. On a semi-log plot, exponential data (y = a*b^x) appears as a straight line because log(y) = log(a) + x*log(b) is linear in x. Key concepts: if data appears linear on a semi-log plot, the original data follows an exponential model; the slope of the semi-log line equals log(b) (the log of the growth factor); the y-intercept equals log(a); semi-log plots are useful for identifying exponential behavior in data that spans many orders of magnitude. Students should read and interpret semi-log plots and convert between semi-log linear relationships and exponential equations. AP exam note: semi-log plots are a distinctive AP Precalculus topic. Students must interpret linearity on a semi-log plot as evidence of exponential behavior. Vocabulary: semi-log plot, logarithmic scale, linear appearance, exponential data, slope, intercept, orders of magnitude.

Trigonometric and Polar Functions.prompt: This topic introduces periodic phenomena for AP Precalculus (CED: 3.1.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.1). A periodic function repeats its values at regular intervals: f(x+p) = f(x) for all x, where p is the period. Key concepts: period is the smallest positive p with this property; periodic phenomena include tides, seasons, sound waves, circular motion, and biological rhythms; amplitude is half the distance between maximum and minimum values; midline is the average of max and min. Students should identify periodic behavior from graphs, tables, and contextual descriptions. This topic motivates the study of trigonometric functions as mathematical models for periodicity. AP exam note: students should identify and describe periodicity in real-world contexts, including period, amplitude, and midline. Vocabulary: periodic function, period, amplitude, midline, cycle, repeating, frequency, oscillation.

This topic covers the sine, cosine, and tangent functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 3.2.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.2). On the unit circle, for angle theta: cos(theta) is the x-coordinate, sin(theta) is the y-coordinate, and tan(theta) = sin(theta)/cos(theta) = y/x. Key concepts: the unit circle definition extends trig functions to all real numbers (not just acute angles); special angles (0, pi/6, pi/4, pi/3, pi/2 and multiples); reference angles for evaluating trig functions in all quadrants; the signs of sin, cos, tan in each quadrant (All Students Take Calculus). Students should evaluate trig functions at special angles without a calculator and understand the unit circle as the foundation. AP exam note: fluency with the unit circle and special angle values is essential for all subsequent trigonometric topics. Vocabulary: unit circle, sine, cosine, tangent, special angles, reference angle, quadrant, radian.

This topic covers evaluating sine and cosine values for AP Precalculus (CED: 3.3.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.3). Students evaluate sin and cos at key angles using the unit circle. Key values: sin(0)=0, sin(pi/6)=1/2, sin(pi/4)=sqrt(2)/2, sin(pi/3)=sqrt(3)/2, sin(pi/2)=1, and corresponding cosine values. Symmetry properties: sin(-theta)=-sin(theta) (odd function), cos(-theta)=cos(theta) (even function). Cofunction identity: sin(pi/2-theta)=cos(theta). Students use reference angles and quadrant signs to evaluate at any angle. Periodicity: sin(theta+2pi)=sin(theta), cos(theta+2pi)=cos(theta). AP exam note: quick, accurate evaluation of trig values is foundational. Students should memorize the unit circle values and apply symmetry properties. Vocabulary: sine values, cosine values, unit circle, special angles, symmetry, odd function, even function, cofunction.

This topic covers graphing sine and cosine functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 3.4.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.4). The parent graphs y = sin(x) and y = cos(x) have period 2pi, amplitude 1, midline y = 0, and range [-1,1]. Key features: sin(x) starts at (0,0) and increases; cos(x) starts at (0,1) and decreases; both are smooth, continuous, wave-shaped curves. The graphs are related by a horizontal shift: cos(x) = sin(x + pi/2). Students should identify key points: maximums, minimums, zeros, and midline crossings. Understanding these parent graphs is essential for analyzing transformations in later topics. AP exam note: students should sketch accurate graphs of sin and cos, label key features, and identify the relationship between the two functions. Vocabulary: sine graph, cosine graph, period, amplitude, midline, range, maximum, minimum, zero, wave.

This topic covers sinusoidal functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 3.5.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.5). A sinusoidal function has the form f(x) = a*sin(b(x-c))+d or f(x) = a*cos(b(x-c))+d. Parameters: |a| = amplitude, 2pi/|b| = period, c = phase shift (horizontal), d = vertical shift (midline y = d). Key concepts: amplitude = (max-min)/2; period = (2pi)/|b|; midline = (max+min)/2 = d; the frequency = |b|/(2pi) gives cycles per unit. Students should determine parameters from graphs, tables, or contextual descriptions and write the equation of a sinusoidal function. If a < 0, the function is reflected. AP exam note: students must extract parameters from various representations and write sinusoidal equations. Vocabulary: sinusoidal, amplitude, period, phase shift, midline, frequency, vertical shift, parameters.

This topic covers transformations of sinusoidal functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 3.6.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.6). Transformations of y = sin(x) or y = cos(x): vertical stretch/compression changes amplitude (|a| in a*sin(x)); horizontal stretch/compression changes period (2pi/b in sin(bx)); horizontal shift (phase shift c in sin(x-c)); vertical shift (midline d in sin(x)+d). Key concepts: transformations combine multiplicatively and additively; the order of transformations matters for correct graphing; students should apply transformations to key points (max, min, zeros, midline crossings) to graph efficiently. Students should be able to both graph a given sinusoidal equation and write the equation from a graph. AP exam note: transformation questions test parameter identification and graphing skills. Students should be efficient with the key-points method. Vocabulary: transformation, amplitude, period, phase shift, vertical shift, key points, stretch, compression.

Trigonometric and Polar Functions.skill: Sinusoidal Function Context and Data Modeling
Trigonometric and Polar Functions.prompt: This topic covers sinusoidal modeling for AP Precalculus (CED: 3.7.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.7). Students model periodic real-world phenomena with sinusoidal functions: temperature over a year, tidal heights, daylight hours, Ferris wheel height, alternating current. Key steps: (1) identify the period from the context; (2) find amplitude = (max-min)/2; (3) find midline = (max+min)/2; (4) determine phase shift from the starting condition; (5) choose sin or cos based on the initial behavior. Students use the model for predictions and interpret results in context. Limitations: real periodic phenomena may not be perfectly sinusoidal. AP exam note: constructing sinusoidal models from contextual descriptions is a core AP Precalculus skill. Students must justify parameter values and interpret predictions. Vocabulary: sinusoidal model, periodic, amplitude, period, midline, phase shift, prediction, context, limitation.

This topic covers the tangent function for AP Precalculus (CED: 3.8.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.8). tan(x) = sin(x)/cos(x), with domain all x except x = pi/2 + n*pi (where cos = 0). Key features: period pi (not 2pi); vertical asymptotes at x = pi/2 + n*pi; range is all reals; the function is increasing on each branch; passes through the origin with slope 1; tan(x) is an odd function (tan(-x) = -tan(x)). The graph consists of repeating branches between consecutive asymptotes. Transformations: a*tan(b(x-c))+d changes the vertical stretch, period (pi/|b|), phase shift, and vertical shift. Students should graph the tangent function and its transformations. AP exam note: students should understand tangent's properties and its relationship to sine and cosine. The period of tangent (pi) differs from sine and cosine (2pi). Vocabulary: tangent function, period pi, vertical asymptote, odd function, increasing, branch, domain restriction.

This topic covers inverse trigonometric functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 3.9.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.9). Since trig functions are periodic and not one-to-one, domains must be restricted for inverses: arcsin(x) (or sin^(-1)(x)) has domain [-1,1] and range [-pi/2, pi/2]; arccos(x) has domain [-1,1] and range [0, pi]; arctan(x) has domain all reals and range (-pi/2, pi/2). Key concepts: arcsin(x) answers 'what angle in [-pi/2, pi/2] has sine x?'; inverse trig functions return angles; f^(-1)(f(x)) = x only on the restricted domain; these functions are essential for solving trigonometric equations. Students should evaluate inverse trig at standard values and compose trig with inverse trig. AP exam note: students must respect the restricted ranges when evaluating inverse trig functions. Vocabulary: inverse trigonometric, arcsin, arccos, arctan, restricted domain, range, principal value.

This topic covers solving trigonometric equations and inequalities for AP Precalculus (CED: 3.10.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.10). To solve trig equations: (1) isolate the trig function; (2) find all solutions in one period using inverse trig and symmetry; (3) add multiples of the period for the general solution. Key concepts: equations like sin(x) = k have infinitely many solutions due to periodicity; the general solution uses n*2pi (for sin and cos) or n*pi (for tan); for equations with transformed arguments (sin(2x) = k), adjust the period accordingly. For inequalities, determine the intervals where the trig function satisfies the condition by analyzing the graph. AP exam note: students should find both specific solutions in an interval and general solutions. Common errors include missing solutions in the given interval. Vocabulary: trigonometric equation, general solution, specific solution, period, inverse trig, inequality, interval.

Trigonometric and Polar Functions.skill: The Secant, Cosecant, and Cotangent Functions
Trigonometric and Polar Functions.prompt: This topic covers secant, cosecant, and cotangent for AP Precalculus (CED: 3.11.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.11). Defined as reciprocals: sec(x) = 1/cos(x), csc(x) = 1/sin(x), cot(x) = cos(x)/sin(x) = 1/tan(x). Key features: vertical asymptotes where the denominator is zero; range excludes (-1,1) for sec and csc; period is 2pi for sec and csc, pi for cot; sec and csc have U-shaped branches; cot is decreasing on each branch. Students should graph these functions and identify key features. Relationships: sec^2(x) = 1 + tan^2(x), csc^2(x) = 1 + cot^2(x) (Pythagorean identities). These functions appear less frequently but are important for trigonometric identities and calculus. AP exam note: students should know the graphs and key features of all six trig functions and their reciprocal relationships. Vocabulary: secant, cosecant, cotangent, reciprocal, vertical asymptote, Pythagorean identity, period.

This topic covers trigonometric identities and equivalent representations for AP Precalculus (CED: 3.12.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.12). Key identities: Pythagorean (sin^2+cos^2=1, tan^2+1=sec^2, 1+cot^2=csc^2); reciprocal; quotient (tan=sin/cos); cofunction; even/odd; sum and difference formulas (sin(A+B), cos(A+B)); double angle formulas (sin(2A)=2sinAcosA, cos(2A)=cos^2A-sin^2A). Students use identities to simplify expressions, verify equivalences, and solve equations. Key skill: choosing the appropriate identity based on the expression's form. AP exam note: students should be able to verify identities by transforming one side to match the other and use identities to solve equations. Vocabulary: identity, Pythagorean identity, sum/difference formula, double angle, verify, simplify, equivalent.

This topic introduces polar coordinates for AP Precalculus (CED: 3.13.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.13). A point in polar coordinates (r, theta) has r = distance from origin and theta = angle from positive x-axis. Conversion: x = r*cos(theta), y = r*sin(theta); r = sqrt(x^2+y^2), theta = arctan(y/x) (adjusted for quadrant). Key concepts: multiple representations (r, theta), (r, theta+2pi), (-r, theta+pi) all represent the same point; the pole (origin) is (0, theta) for any theta; polar coordinates are natural for describing rotational symmetry, circular motion, and radial distances. Students should convert between rectangular and polar coordinates and plot points in polar. AP exam note: polar coordinate fluency is essential for graphing polar functions and computing polar areas (in calculus). Vocabulary: polar coordinates, radius, angle, conversion, rectangular, pole, radian, multiple representations.

This topic covers graphing polar functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 3.14.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.14). Polar curves r = f(theta) are graphed by computing r for various theta values and plotting (r, theta) points. Common curves: circles (r = a, r = a*cos(theta)), cardioids (r = a + a*cos(theta)), limacons (r = a + b*cos(theta)), roses (r = a*cos(n*theta) or a*sin(n*theta) with n petals for odd n, 2n for even n), spirals (r = a*theta). Key concepts: symmetry tests (about polar axis, about pi/2 line, about pole); finding where r = 0 (curve passes through pole); understanding how negative r values are plotted. Students should sketch polar curves by hand and with technology. AP exam note: students should identify curve types from equations and sketch accurate graphs. Vocabulary: polar curve, cardioid, limacon, rose, spiral, symmetry, petal, polar axis, negative r.

Trigonometric and Polar Functions.skill: Rates of Change in Polar Functions
Trigonometric and Polar Functions.prompt: This topic covers rates of change in polar functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 3.15.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.15). For r = f(theta), dr/d(theta) represents the rate at which the distance from the origin changes with respect to the angle. Key concepts: dr/d(theta) > 0 means r is increasing (curve moves away from pole); dr/d(theta) < 0 means r is decreasing (curve moves toward pole); dr/d(theta) = 0 may correspond to maximum or minimum distance from the origin. Students analyze how the polar curve's distance from the origin changes as theta varies, connecting this to the shape of the curve. The average rate of change of r over an interval of theta can be computed from the function. AP exam note: students should interpret dr/d(theta) in the context of polar curve behavior and connect it to the visual shape of the graph. Vocabulary: rate of change, polar function, dr/dtheta, increasing, decreasing, distance from origin, maximum distance.

Functions Involving Parameters, Vectors,.prompt: This topic introduces parametric functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 4.1.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.1). A parametric function defines x and y each as functions of a parameter t: x = f(t), y = g(t). As t varies, the point (x,y) traces a curve in the plane. Key concepts: the parameter t often represents time; the same curve can have different parametrizations; parametric representation can describe curves that are not functions of x (e.g., circles, loops); eliminating the parameter gives the Cartesian equation. Students should graph parametric curves by making a table of (t, x, y) values and plotting, and understand how the parameter determines direction of travel. AP exam note: students should graph parametric curves and understand the role of the parameter in determining direction and speed along the curve. Vocabulary: parametric function, parameter, trace, curve, direction, eliminate parameter, Cartesian equation.

This topic covers using parametric functions to model planar motion for AP Precalculus (CED: 4.2.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.2). When an object moves in a plane, its position at time t is given by x(t) and y(t). Key concepts: x(t) describes horizontal position and y(t) describes vertical position; the path traced is the trajectory; direction of motion is determined by increasing t; speed relates to how fast x and y change with t. Common models: projectile motion (x = v0*cos(theta)*t, y = v0*sin(theta)*t - (1/2)g*t^2), uniform circular motion (x = r*cos(omega*t), y = r*sin(omega*t)). Students should analyze motion from parametric equations and interpret in context. AP exam note: students must describe the motion (direction, speed, position) from parametric equations and connect to real-world contexts. Vocabulary: planar motion, trajectory, projectile, circular motion, position, direction, parameter t, horizontal, vertical.

Functions Involving Parameters, Vectors,.skill: Parametric Functions and Rates of Change
Functions Involving Parameters, Vectors,.prompt: This topic covers rates of change for parametric functions in AP Precalculus (CED: 4.3.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.3). For parametric equations x(t), y(t), the average rate of change of y with respect to x over an interval is Delta_y/Delta_x = [y(t2)-y(t1)]/[x(t2)-x(t1)]. Key concepts: this ratio gives the slope of the secant line on the parametric curve between two points; if both x and y are differentiable, the instantaneous rate dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt); the rates dx/dt and dy/dt represent horizontal and vertical velocities. Students should compute and interpret average rates of change from parametric data and understand how horizontal and vertical rates combine to describe motion along the curve. AP exam note: students should compute average rates of change of y with respect to x from parametric data and interpret them as slopes. Vocabulary: rate of change, parametric, slope, secant line, horizontal rate, vertical rate, average rate.

This topic covers parametric representations of circles and lines for AP Precalculus (CED: 4.4.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.4). Line: x = x0 + at, y = y0 + bt traces a line through (x0,y0) with direction vector <a,b>. Circle: x = h + r*cos(t), y = k + r*sin(t) traces a circle centered at (h,k) with radius r. Key concepts: the parameter t determines position and direction of travel; for circles, t represents the angle; the line parametrization can be restricted to a segment by limiting t; eliminating t from the circle parametrization gives (x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 = r^2. Students should write parametric equations for given circles and lines and eliminate parameters to verify Cartesian equations. AP exam note: students should fluently convert between parametric and Cartesian forms for circles and lines. Vocabulary: parametric circle, parametric line, direction vector, center, radius, eliminate parameter, direction of travel.

This topic covers implicitly defined functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 4.5.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.5). An implicit equation like x^2 + y^2 = 25 defines a relationship between x and y without explicitly solving for one variable. Key concepts: implicit equations can define curves that are not functions (failing the vertical line test); solving for y may yield multiple branches, each of which may be a function; analyzing implicit equations involves identifying symmetry, intercepts, and other features directly from the equation. Common implicit curves: circles, ellipses, hyperbolas, and more complex relations. Students should graph implicit equations using technology and analyze their features. AP exam note: students should identify key features of implicitly defined curves (symmetry, intercepts, domain) without necessarily solving for y. Vocabulary: implicit equation, explicit function, relation, curve, symmetry, intercept, branch, vertical line test.

This topic covers parametrizing implicitly defined curves for AP Precalculus (CED: 4.7.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.7). Students convert implicit equations into parametric form. Key examples: circle x^2+y^2=r^2 becomes x=r*cos(t), y=r*sin(t); ellipse x^2/a^2+y^2/b^2=1 becomes x=a*cos(t), y=b*sin(t). Key concepts: parametrization adds direction and timing information to an implicit curve; the same implicit curve can have multiple parametrizations (different speeds, directions, starting points); choosing the right parametrization simplifies analysis and computation. Students should find parametric equations for standard implicit curves and understand the role of the parameter. AP exam note: students should convert between implicit and parametric forms and understand the advantages of each representation. Vocabulary: parametrize, implicit curve, parametric form, direction, speed, ellipse, circle, parameter.

This topic covers conic sections for AP Precalculus (CED: 4.6.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.6). Conics are curves formed by intersecting a plane with a cone: circles, ellipses, parabolas, and hyperbolas. Standard forms: circle (x-h)^2+(y-k)^2=r^2; ellipse (x-h)^2/a^2+(y-k)^2/b^2=1; parabola y-k=a(x-h)^2 or x-h=a(y-k)^2; hyperbola (x-h)^2/a^2-(y-k)^2/b^2=1. Key features: center, vertices, foci, asymptotes (hyperbolas), directrix (parabolas), eccentricity. Students should identify, graph, and write equations for each conic type. The general second-degree equation Ax^2+Bxy+Cy^2+Dx+Ey+F=0 can represent any conic. AP exam note: students should identify conic type from equations and know key features for each. Vocabulary: conic section, circle, ellipse, parabola, hyperbola, focus, vertex, asymptote, eccentricity, standard form.

This topic introduces vectors for AP Precalculus (CED: 4.8.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.8). A vector has magnitude (length) and direction, written as <a,b> or ai+bj. Key operations: addition (<a,b>+<c,d>=<a+c,b+d>), scalar multiplication (k<a,b>=<ka,kb>), magnitude |v|=sqrt(a^2+b^2), direction angle theta=arctan(b/a). Unit vector: v/|v|. Key concepts: vectors represent displacement, velocity, force, and other directed quantities; the zero vector has magnitude 0; vectors can be added geometrically (tip-to-tail) or algebraically (component-wise); the dot product u*v = |u||v|cos(theta) = a1a2+b1b2 measures alignment. Students should perform vector operations, find magnitude and direction, and interpret vectors in context. AP exam note: students should be comfortable with component form and geometric interpretation of vectors. Vocabulary: vector, magnitude, direction, component, unit vector, scalar multiplication, addition, dot product.

This topic covers vector-valued functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 4.9.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.9). A vector-valued function r(t) = <x(t), y(t)> assigns a vector to each value of t, tracing a curve in the plane. Key concepts: r(t) gives position at time t; the difference r(t2)-r(t1) gives the displacement vector; average velocity = (r(t2)-r(t1))/(t2-t1); the direction of motion is given by the direction of the displacement or velocity vector. Vector-valued functions are essentially parametric equations expressed in vector notation. Students should graph the path, determine direction of travel, compute displacement, and interpret in context. AP exam note: students should connect vector-valued function notation to parametric equations and interpret motion in context. Vocabulary: vector-valued function, position vector, displacement, average velocity, direction, parametric, trace.

This topic introduces matrices for AP Precalculus (CED: 4.10.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.10). A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers with m rows and n columns (m x n). Key operations: addition (same dimensions, add corresponding entries), scalar multiplication (multiply each entry by the scalar), matrix multiplication (AB defined when columns of A = rows of B; entry (i,j) of AB = dot product of row i of A and column j of B). Key concepts: matrix multiplication is not commutative (AB != BA in general); the identity matrix I (diagonal of 1s) satisfies AI = IA = A; matrices can represent systems of linear equations, transformations, and data. Students should perform matrix operations and understand their properties. AP exam note: students should compute matrix operations accurately, especially matrix multiplication, and understand when operations are defined. Vocabulary: matrix, dimension, row, column, addition, scalar multiplication, matrix multiplication, identity matrix, entry.

Functions Involving Parameters, Vectors,.skill: The Inverse and Determinant of a Matrix
Functions Involving Parameters, Vectors,.prompt: This topic covers matrix inverses and determinants for AP Precalculus (CED: 4.11.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.11). For a 2x2 matrix A = [[a,b],[c,d]], the determinant is det(A) = ad-bc. A is invertible if and only if det(A) != 0, and A^(-1) = (1/det(A))*[[d,-b],[-c,a]]. Key concepts: AA^(-1) = A^(-1)A = I (identity); the inverse is used to solve matrix equations AX = B as X = A^(-1)B; det(A) = 0 means the system has no unique solution (singular matrix); geometric interpretation: |det(A)| gives the area scaling factor of the linear transformation represented by A. Students should compute determinants and inverses for 2x2 matrices and apply inverses to solve systems. AP exam note: students should compute 2x2 determinants and inverses and use them to solve systems of equations. Vocabulary: determinant, inverse matrix, singular, invertible, identity, system of equations, area scaling.

This topic covers linear transformations represented by matrices for AP Precalculus (CED: 4.12.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.12). A 2x2 matrix A represents a linear transformation T: R^2 -> R^2 by T(v) = Av. Key transformations: rotation by angle theta: [[cos(theta),-sin(theta)],[sin(theta),cos(theta)]]; reflection over x-axis: [[1,0],[0,-1]]; scaling: [[k,0],[0,k]]; shear: [[1,k],[0,1]]. Key concepts: linear transformations preserve the origin, lines, and parallelism; the columns of the matrix are the images of the standard basis vectors; composition of transformations corresponds to matrix multiplication; the determinant gives the area scaling factor (negative det means orientation reversal). Students should identify and apply standard transformations and compose them. AP exam note: students should connect matrix entries to geometric transformations and understand how composition works via multiplication. Vocabulary: linear transformation, matrix representation, rotation, reflection, scaling, shear, composition, basis vectors.

This topic covers interpreting matrices as functions for AP Precalculus (CED: 4.13.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.13). A matrix A defines a function T_A: R^n -> R^m by T_A(x) = Ax. Key concepts: the input is a vector, the output is a vector; this function is linear (T(u+v) = T(u)+T(v), T(kv) = kT(v)); the matrix encodes how inputs map to outputs; inverse matrices define inverse functions; composing matrix functions corresponds to multiplying matrices. Students should evaluate matrix functions at specific inputs, compose them, and find inverses when they exist. This perspective unifies matrices with the function concept studied throughout the course. AP exam note: students should treat matrices as functions, evaluating them at vector inputs and understanding composition as multiplication. Vocabulary: matrix function, linear function, input vector, output vector, composition, inverse, evaluate.

This topic covers using matrices to model real-world contexts for AP Precalculus (CED: 4.14.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.14). Matrices model: systems of linear equations (Ax = b), transition matrices (Markov chains, population movement between states), economic models (input-output analysis), network models (adjacency matrices for graphs), and data organization. Key concepts: setting up the matrix equation from a contextual description; interpreting matrix multiplication in context (e.g., for transition matrices, A^n gives the state after n steps); understanding what the entries of the product matrix represent. Students should construct matrix models from descriptions and interpret results in context. AP exam note: students must translate contextual problems into matrix form, compute products, and interpret the results meaningfully. Vocabulary: matrix model, system of equations, transition matrix, Markov chain, network, adjacency, context, interpret.
